title: Day 26: Becoming a Team That Wins Together

steps.title: From Partners to Teammates
steps.body:
There's a difference between being partners and being teammates.

Partners share a life. Teammates share a mission. They strategize together, cover each other's weaknesses, and celebrate each other's wins.

In a great marriage team:
• You don't keep score — because the team wins or loses together
• You have different roles — but everyone's role matters
• You communicate — because the team breaks without it
• You celebrate each other — because your success is the team's success
• You have each other's back — even when your partner is the one who made the mistake

Today: how to function as a real team, not just two people sharing a zip code.

Your partner is not your opponent.
Your partner is your teammate.

The problem isn't the problem.
The problem is how you handle the problem as a team.
highlights: handle the problem as a team

steps.title: Are You Acting Like a Team?

questions.label:
Think about the last stressful situation you and your partner faced.

Did you tackle it together — 'us against the problem'? Or did it become 'me against you'?

When something goes wrong in your household, whose fault is it — or do you look at the system that failed?

Rate your marriage as a team: 1 (we're opposing players) to 10 (we're a championship team). What would move the needle?

steps.title: The Signals of Teamwork vs Rivalry
steps.body:
Teamwork language:
• 'We need to figure this out.'
• 'How can I support you right now?'
• 'That's hard — let's tackle it together.'
• 'I trust your judgment on this.'

Rivalry language:
• 'Your turn.'
• 'Well, I did X, so you need to do Y.'
• 'Why can't you just...'
• 'If you'd done your part...'

Notice the difference. Teamwork language is collaborative. Rivalry language is transactional.

The goal isn't to eliminate rivalry language. It's to notice when you're speaking it and course-correct.

steps.question: Which of these statements reflects a team mindset?
options: 'I did the dishes, so you owe me one.'
'This week has been brutal. Can we divide and conquer — I'll handle dinners, can you handle the kids' school stuff?'
'You're not pulling your weight around here.'
'Fine, I'll do it myself like always.'
feedback: That's scorekeeping, not teamwork. Teammates don't track debts.
Yes. This is strategic collaboration — looking at the shared load and dividing it based on capacity, not obligation.
Accusation, not collaboration. A team approach would be: 'I'm feeling the load is uneven — can we talk about how to balance it?'
Resignation isn't teamwork. It's resentment wearing a 'martyr' mask.

steps.title: The Team Huddle
steps.body:
Elite sports teams huddle. They pause, look each other in the eye, and get on the same page before the next play.

Your marriage needs huddles too.

A weekly huddle could be as simple as:

1. What went well this week? (Win)
2. What felt hard? (Challenge)
3. What do we need from each other next week? (Plan)

That's 15 minutes. No fixing. No debating. Just alignment.

Teams that huddle win more games. Couples who huddle feel more connected.

steps.title: Hold Your First Huddle
steps.prompt:
This week, hold a 15-minute 'team huddle' with your partner.

Structure:
1. Win — 'Something you did this week I appreciated:'
2. Challenge — 'Something that was hard:'
3. Plan — 'What I need from you next week:'

Go around for each. No cross-talk. No arguing. Just sharing.

Write your proposed time and how you'll invite your partner:
steps.body:
This week, hold a 15-minute 'team huddle' with your partner.

Structure:
1. Win — 'Something you did this week I appreciated:'
2. Challenge — 'Something that was hard:'
3. Plan — 'What I need from you next week:'

Go around for each. No cross-talk. No arguing. Just sharing.

Write your proposed time and how you'll invite your partner:

questions.label:
Every team has moments where they lose coordination. A dropped ball. A miscommunication. A 'I thought YOU were handling that.'

When that happens in your marriage, what's your reflex?

Do you blame, or do you recalibrate?

What would it look like to say: 'We dropped the ball on that one. That's on us as a team. How do we not miss it next time?'

Write your honest reflex — and how you'd like to respond instead.

steps.question: In a strong marriage team, each partner should be equally good at everything.
feedback: Correct. Great teams don't need every player to do everything. They need each player to play their position. Know your strengths, know your partner's weaknesses, and cover for each other.
Not quite. Great teams work through complementary strengths, not identical skill sets. The key is knowing who's good at what and trusting each other to handle their role.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Celebrating and Savoring
steps.body:
Tomorrow, something different. We've talked about hard things — repair, boundaries, growth. But thriving marriages also know how to celebrate. How to savor good moments. How to let joy land.

Tomorrow: the underrated skill of celebrating your marriage.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
